step.title: Step 14
step.actionDisplay: RIGHT -> MOUSE(row=4, col=37) -> RIGHT -> LEFT -> UP -> DOWN

sections.label: USER PROMPT
sections.content:
The code executed 6 actions in the previous sequence.
Executed actions: RIGHT, MOUSE(row=4, col=37), RIGHT, LEFT, UP, DOWN.
You are still on the same level.
Current state: step 38, level 1.
Valid actions right now: UP, DOWN, LEFT, RIGHT, SPACE, MOUSE.
Only tool: `python`. It receives `current_frame`, `previous_frame`, `history`, `transitions`, `last_transition`, `valid_actions`, `last_action_result`, and `action(actions)`. It also provides `last_animation` (per-action frames of your last `action(...)` call) and `frame_stats` (how much this game has animated so far) -- optional; use them only if you judge the intermediate motion informative rather than incidental. You may also `predict(expect_change=..., expected_grid=...)` before an `action(...)` to test your world model; `last_prediction_result`/`prediction_stats` then score it, and a `surprise` flags where your model was wrong -- optional, use it when you hold a belief worth checking.
Only letter-coded board views and lightweight metadata are exposed; raw numeric color IDs are not available.
Keep tool output compact: use `current_frame.segmentation` as the primary view, and `current_frame.ascii` only for a small specific region; never print full boards.
For the most recent change, compare `previous_frame` to `current_frame`, or `last_transition.before_frame` to `last_transition.after_frame`; `history[-1].frame` is the current frame, not the previous one.
Use Python to inspect the evidence, refine that world model from the newest history, and search or score candidate actions or short sequences against the current goal as you currently understand it.
Maintain a compact working world model of what the current level seems to contain, what actions appear to do, what the goal seems to be, what is still uncertain, and what plan currently looks best.
Below you are provided with the current world model from the previous turn. The default behavior is to copy it and add or remove things based on the evidence that you gathered. BEFORE EXECUTING NEW ACTIONS YOU MUST ALWAYS GIVE THE REVISED VERSION OF THE WORLD MODEL.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it, but stop immediately if a result reports `game_over`, `run_complete`, `level_completed`, or `done`.
end of world model. 
Focus on what changed most recently in `history`, update the target environment change if needed, and separate gameplay-object changes from HUD-only changes.
When ready, call `action(actions)` from inside the `python` tool with the best valid action or ordered batch selected by your code. If your code has found a reliable short sequence, prefer batching it in one call.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it.
If you include assistant text before a tool call, keep it short and use it to update the world model. Helpful optional prefixes are `World model:`, `Goal model:`, `Action model:`, `Recent findings:`, `Open questions:`, `Plan:`, and `Cross-level notes:`.
When calling `python`, emit exactly the tool-call format shown elsewhere in this prompt for this model. Use only that format; do not add markdown fences, prose wrappers, or alternate tool-call syntax. Do not quote or place tool-call markup inside explanatory text; when you decide to call the tool, emit the tool call itself.
If you use MOUSE, include integer row and col arguments.
EFFICIENCY BUDGET — your score on each level is (baseline_actions / your_actions)^2, so every wasted action costs you quadratically.
Level 1: you have used 37 of about 55 target actions so far.

sections.label: THINKING
sections.content:
OK so UP/DOWN/LEFT/RIGHT don't move the selector at all. The selector only moves when I MOUSE click on the legend boxes. LEFT/RIGHT only change the top block's shape.
Let me reconsider the problem completely. I've been using 37 actions and I'm running out of the 55 target budget. Let me think about what the simplest solution might be.
Current state:
- Top block: white rectangle
- Bottom block: white rectangle
- Legend: white on top, purple on bottom
What if the goal is simply to make the bottom block match the legend (white-top, purple-bottom)? But I can't figure out how to get purple on the bottom half.
Wait, let me reconsider the stamping mechanic. When I stamp purple with a rectangle, it fills the TOP 50px. When I stamp white with a rectangle, it fills the ENTIRE 100px. What if the fill direction depends on the color? Purple fills from top, white fills from bottom? No, that doesn't match - white filled the entire block.
Actually, wait. Let me reconsider. What if:
- Purple stamp: fills 50px from the TOP
- White stamp: fills 50px from the BOTTOM
But when I stamped white on a block that was purple-top/white-bottom, the result was all white (100px). That's because the bottom 50px were already white, and the stamp filled the bottom 50px with white (no change) plus... no, that doesn't work.
Let me re-examine. When the bottom block was all white and I stamped white, the result was still all white (100px). When it was purple-top/white-bottom and I stamped white, the result was all white (100px). So white stamp ALWAYS results in 100px white.
But purple stamp on a white block gives 50px purple + 50px white. And purple stamp on a purple-top/white-bottom block gives... I haven't tried that.
Let me try: stamp purple on a block that already has purple on top. Does it extend the purple region?
Actually, let me try a completely different approach. What if I need to stamp purple, then stamp white, then stamp purple again? Or some other sequence?
Or what if the goal isn't about the bottom block at all? What if the goal is to make the TOP block match the legend? The legend shows a white rectangle on top and a purple rectangle on bottom. But the top block is a single object, not two stacked objects.
Hmm, let me look at this from a different angle. What if the legend shows the TWO selector boxes, and the goal is to use them to paint the game objects to match some target? But what target?
Let me try to look at the initial state more carefully. The initial state had:
- Top block: purple rectangle
- Bottom block: white rectangle
And the legend shows:
- White on top
- Purple on bottom
So the legend is the REVERSE of the initial state. Maybe the goal is to swap the colors:
- Top block should be white
- Bottom block should be purple
To achieve this, I need:
1. Top block = white (just select white)
2. Bottom block = purple (stamp purple... but it only fills top half)
What if I need to stamp purple twice? Let me try:
1. Select purple, SPACE -> top half purple
2. Select purple, SPACE again -> ???

sections.label: TOOL CALL: python
sections.content:
<tool_call>
<function=python>
<parameter=code>
# Let me try stamping purple twice to see if it extends
# First reset bottom to white
action([{'action': 'MOUSE', 'row': 4, 'col': 37}])  # select white
action(['SPACE'])  # fill bottom with white

# Now stamp purple twice
action([{'action': 'MOUSE', 'row': 4, 'col': 43}])  # select purple
action(['SPACE'])  # first purple stamp

seg = current_frame.segmentation
print("After first purple stamp:")
for n in seg['nodes']:
    if n['id'] >= 12:
        print(f"  id={n['id']} color={n['color']} pixels={n['pixels']} boundary={n['boundary']}")

action(['SPACE'])  # second purple stamp

seg = current_frame.segmentation
print("\nAfter second purple stamp:")
for n in seg['nodes']:
    if n['id'] >= 12:
        print(f"  id={n['id']} color={n['color']} pixels={n['pixels']} boundary={n['boundary']}")
</parameter>
</function>
</tool_call>

sections.content:

  id=12 color=p pixels=84 boundary=[[25, 26], [25, 37], [31, 37], [31, 26]]
  id=13 color=p pixels=50 boundary=[[34, 27], [34, 36], [38, 36], [38, 27]]
  id=14 color=W pixels=50 boundary=[[39, 27], [39, 36], [43, 36], [43, 27]]
  id=15 color=c pixels=38 boundary=[[63, 0], [63, 37]]

After second purple stamp:
  id=12 color=p pixels=84 boundary=[[25, 26], [25, 37], [31, 37], [31, 26]]
  id=13 color=p pixels=50 boundary=[[34, 27], [34, 36], [38, 36], [38, 27]]
  id=14 color=W pixels=50 boundary=[[39, 27], [39, 36], [43, 36], [43, 27]]
  id=15 color=c pixels=37 boundary=[[63, 0], [63, 36]]
